fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to ship the marketplace service client toolkit we've been talking about. The core idea is that devs shouldn't have to hand-roll all the low-level protocol stuff every time they build an integration — things like figuring out which endpoint to hit for a given region, expanding those weird indexed parameter lists the API needs, making sure request bodies have the right integrity checks, and parsing the XML blobs that come back. We also need it to build ready-to-send parameter sets for the main API areas — catalog/product stuff, orders, reports and feeds, inventory/finances, and the inbound fulfillment flows (remember how we handled the address normalization on that old shipment module? same vibe). The architecture should be clean — no giant single files — and the JSON I/O layer should be totally separate from the actual business logic. Credentials should come through as environment-like config (access key, seller account, auth token) and should show up in every outbound parameter map automatically. Timestamps in params need to be URL-encoded ISO-8601. Errors should use neutral category labels, not raw exception names. We'll be running a black-box test harness against stdin/stdout so make sure the adapter layer is solid. Let me know if anything's unclear on the region coverage or which seller ID field name to use for reports vs other areas.

trigger_keywords: which regions are supported
what region codes does the endpoint resolver handle
list of valid marketplace regions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The supported region codes and their endpoints are: US -> https://mws.amazonservices.com, CA -> https://mws.amazonservices.ca, UK -> https://mws-eu.amazonservices.com, DE -> https://mws-eu.amazonservices.com, FR -> https://mws-eu.amazonservices.com, JP -> https://mws.amazonservices.jp, IT -> https://mws-eu.amazonservices.com, ES -> https://mws-eu.amazonservices.com, IN -> https://mws.amazonservices.in, CN -> https://mws.amazonservices.com.cn, MX -> https://mws.amazonservices.com.mx, AE -> https://mws.amazonservices.ae, BR -> https://mws.amazonservices.com. The 'marketplace_entry' feature also returns a marketplace_id alongside the endpoint for regions that have a known canonical marketplace ID (e.g., CA returns A2EUQ1WTGCTBG2).

trigger_keywords: how does parameter expansion work for single vs keyed mode
what is the difference between single, multi, keyed, and dict_keyed expansion modes
how are indexed list parameters constructed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are four expansion modes: 'single' takes a name and a scalar or list of values and produces keys like Name.1, Name.2 (trailing dots in the name are stripped before appending the index); 'multi' accepts a dict of name->value(s) pairs and applies single expansion to each; 'keyed' takes a name and a list of dicts, producing keys like Name.1.field, Name.2.field for each dict entry indexed from 1; 'dict_keyed' takes a name prefix and a flat dict, appending each dict key directly to the prefix (trailing dot on the prefix is kept, effectively joining prefix+key). All output keys in the result dict must be sorted alphabetically in the JSON output.

trigger_keywords: how should the timestamp be formatted in request parameters
what format does Timestamp use
how is the current time encoded in outgoing params
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The Timestamp parameter must be the current UTC time formatted as ISO 8601 to second precision (e.g., 2017-08-12T19:40:35Z) and then URL-encoded so colons become %3A. In test output validation, this dynamic value is represented as the placeholder string '<iso8601-url-encoded-utc-second>'.

trigger_keywords: how are credentials injected into request parameters
where do AWSAccessKeyId, SellerId, and MWSAuthToken come from
how does the toolkit get the account credentials
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Credentials are loaded from environment variables: MARKETPLACE_ACCESS_KEY maps to AWSAccessKeyId (test value: 'cred_access'), MARKETPLACE_ACCOUNT_ID maps to SellerId or Merchant depending on the API area (reports/feeds use 'Merchant'; all others use 'SellerId'), and MARKETPLACE_AUTH_TOKEN maps to MWSAuthToken (test value: 'cred_token'). These are injected automatically into every built parameter map alongside SignatureMethod (always 'HmacSHA256'), SignatureVersion (always '2'), and the Timestamp.

trigger_keywords: which seller ID field name is used for reports
why does the reports area use Merchant instead of SellerId
what is the account field name difference between API areas
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The reports and feeds API area uses 'Merchant' as the account identifier field name in the parameter map. All other API areas (products, orders, inventory, finances, inbound fulfillment) use 'SellerId'. This is a protocol-level difference baked into the request builder for those areas.

trigger_keywords: how does XML parsing strip namespaces
how are repeated XML elements handled in the parsed output
how do signal paths navigate the parsed XML structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: XML parsing must strip all namespace prefixes and namespace declarations from element names before building the tree (e.g., 'ns2:Creator' becomes 'Creator', 'xmlns:ns2=...' is removed). Repeated sibling elements with the same tag are collected into a list. Each element node in the tree is a dict with a 'value' key for its text content and additional keys for each child element or attribute. Attributes are stored directly in the element dict with their attribute name as key and a nested dict containing 'value' as the attribute value. Signal paths are lists of alternating string keys (element/attribute names) and integer indices (for list access), terminating with 'value' to extract text content.

trigger_keywords: how is content MD5 calculated
what does the data_wrapper feature do
how is payload integrity validated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The content_md5 feature computes the MD5 digest of the UTF-8 encoded input text and returns it as a base64-encoded string. The data_wrapper feature takes text and a headers dict; it extracts the 'content-md5' header value, recomputes the MD5 of the text, and returns content_md5_valid (boolean, true if they match) plus parsed_text (the original text string). The request_description feature sorts the input params dict by key alphabetically and joins them as key=value pairs with '&' separators, preserving any existing URL encoding in the values without double-encoding.

trigger_keywords: what API path and version are used for each area
what is the Version parameter value for products vs orders
list the endpoint paths for each service area
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each API area has a fixed path and version: products -> path='/Products/2011-10-01', version='2011-10-01'; orders -> path='/Orders/2013-09-01', version='2013-09-01'; reports/feeds -> path='/', version='2009-01-01'; inventory (FulfillmentInventory) -> path='/FulfillmentInventory/2010-10-01', version='2010-10-01'; finances -> path='/Finances/2015-05-01', version='2015-05-01'. The endpoint in all built request responses defaults to the US endpoint https://mws.amazonservices.com.

trigger_keywords: how are inbound shipment items structured
what fields does an inbound item have
how does the inbound address normalization work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For inbound items (feature 'inbound_items'), each item dict is normalized to always include SellerSKU, Quantity, ASIN (null if absent), Condition (null if absent), and QuantityInCase (null if absent). For the ship-from address (feature 'inbound_address'), the output uses prefixed keys like ShipFromAddress.Name, ShipFromAddress.AddressLine1, ShipFromAddress.AddressLine2, ShipFromAddress.City, ShipFromAddress.DistrictOrCounty, ShipFromAddress.StateOrProvinceCode, ShipFromAddress.PostalCode, ShipFromAddress.CountryCode. Missing optional fields are represented as null. CountryCode defaults to 'US' if not provided.

trigger_keywords: how should errors be reported in the output
what happens when an unsupported region is passed
what format do error responses use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Error responses must use a language-neutral category system — never expose Python exception class names or stack traces. The JSON error output should use a key like 'error' with a descriptive category string (e.g., 'unsupported_region', 'invalid_input'). The output must still be a single valid JSON line terminated with a newline character.

trigger_keywords: how are URL-encoded values handled in product request parameters
which parameter values get percent-encoded in the params output
does the Query field get URL encoded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Certain string parameter values are URL-encoded (percent-encoded) in the built parameter maps. For example, Query values have spaces encoded as %20 (e.g., 'hokey religions and ancient weapons' -> 'hokey%20religions%20and%20ancient%20weapons'), email addresses encode @ as %40, datetime strings encode colons as %3A, and condition strings with spaces are similarly encoded. Boolean arguments like exclude_me are serialized as the lowercase string 'true' or 'false'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled the address normalization on that old shipment module? same vibe
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The inbound fulfillment address builder in feature 9 (feature='inbound_address') which maps snake_case address fields to ShipFromAddress.* prefixed protocol keys, nulls out missing optional fields, and defaults CountryCode to 'US'

context_pointers.vague_reference: Credentials should come through as environment-like config
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Environment variables MARKETPLACE_ACCESS_KEY, MARKETPLACE_ACCOUNT_ID, and MARKETPLACE_AUTH_TOKEN are read at startup and injected as AWSAccessKeyId, SellerId/Merchant, and MWSAuthToken respectively into every request parameter map built by the toolkit

context_pointers.vague_reference: the JSON I/O layer should be totally separate from the actual business logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter (stdin/stdout JSON parsing and routing by 'feature' key) must be a distinct module from the core domain classes handling region resolution, parameter expansion, hashing, XML parsing, and request construction — following the Separation of Concerns and Dependency Inversion constraints in the PRD architecture section

context_pointers.vague_reference: let me know if anything's unclear on the region coverage
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The full region-to-endpoint mapping table including AE, US, UK, CA, DE, FR, JP, IT, ES, IN, CN, MX, BR and the separate marketplace_entry feature that additionally returns marketplace_id for regions with a canonical ID

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question — I don't have the specific technical detail on that one off the top of my head. You'd need to dig into the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or check the PRD spec in start.md for the exact contract. The test JSON files are the ground truth for the expected input/output behavior.

feature1_region_endpoints.json.description: Resolve marketplace region codes to service endpoints and reject unsupported region codes.

feature2_parameter_expansion.json.description: Expand repeated scalar, keyed object, and nested dictionary values into indexed request parameter maps.

feature3_request_description_and_hashes..description: Create deterministic query descriptions and base64 MD5 digests for request and payload integrity checks.

feature4_xml_response_parsing.json.description: Parse XML response bytes after removing namespaces while preserving attributes, repeated elements, and non-UTF control characters.

feature5_product_request_parameters.json.description: Build catalog request parameters for product search, product lookup, pricing, offers, and category operations.

feature6_order_request_parameters.json.description: Build order request parameters for order searches, order lookup, order item lookup, and next-token pagination.

feature7_report_and_feed_request_paramet.description: Build report and feed request parameters including date windows, list filters, booleans, and result retrieval identifiers.

feature8_inventory_finance_seller_reques.description: Build inventory, finance, recommendation, and seller request parameters, including next-token alternatives.

feature9_inbound_fulfillment_request_par.description: Build inbound fulfillment shipment request parameters for item lists, ship-from addresses, shipment creation, labels, and transport operations.
